name: feat-ESE-0012
title: GPU compute system for ECS
description:
Add a GPU compute system to the ECS engine, enabling systems to run on the GPU via WebGPU compute shaders. SoA TypedArray component storage uploads directly as GPU storage buffers. A kernel DSL generates WGSL from component schemas. Shared components (e.g. Transform) work across CPU and GPU systems via ownership-based sync at phase boundaries.

Reference implementations studied: TypeGPU, Use.GPU, bevy_gpu_compute, bevy_hanabi, Unity GPU Physics, NVIDIA GPU Gems Ch.32. See references/ directory for insights.
acceptanceCriteria:
- GpuContext manages WebGPU device, queue, and buffer pool
- GpuKernelDef DSL generates valid WGSL from component schemas
- Buffer sync uploads dirty CPU buffers before dispatch, reads back GPU-written buffers after
- Particle system PoC: 10k+ particles simulated on GPU, rendered via existing Three.js path
- GPU and CPU systems can share components (e.g. Transform) without data corruption
- Multi-pass physics: broadphase spatial hash + narrowphase collision + Verlet integration on GPU
demoDeliverable: Browser demo showing 10,000+ GPU-simulated particles with real-time physics. Console log shows GPU dispatch timing vs equivalent CPU system timing. A second scene demonstrates GPU collision detection between rigid bodies with shared Transform components read by the existing CPU rendering pipeline.
testingScenarios:
- Unit tests for WGSL code generation from component schemas
- Unit tests for buffer sync (upload/readback correctness)
- Integration test: GPU particle system matches CPU reference implementation within epsilon
- Integration test: GPU physics collision detection finds same pairs as brute-force CPU reference
- Stress test: 100k particles at stable frame rate
stakeholderUnderstanding: This feature adds GPU compute to our ECS engine. Our SoA TypedArray storage maps directly to GPU storage buffers — no data reshaping needed. A kernel DSL generates WGSL from component schemas so system authors never write binding boilerplate. Shared components (Transform, Velocity) work across CPU and GPU systems via ownership-based sync at pipeline phase boundaries. All new GPU code uses the 'gpu' prefix to avoid blocking existing CPU-bound work. The particle PoC proves the stack end-to-end; the physics ticket proves multi-pass dispatch for real workloads.
